summary: Jeff Atwood announces his keynote at EclipseCon 2009 with Clay Shirky, discussing the theory and practice of online community design. He reveals that Shirky's book 'Here Comes Everybody' served as a blueprint for building Stack Overflow. Atwood expresses deep admiration for Shirky's work on internet culture and community. He also emphasizes his satisfaction in presenting to non-Windows developers, as Stack Overflow was always meant to serve the broader programming community regardless of platform or toolchain.
key_points: Clay Shirky's 'Here Comes Everybody' was used as a literal blueprint for building Stack Overflow
Stack Overflow was intentionally designed as a cross-platform community celebrating the craft of programming, not tied to any specific technology
The talk pairs theory (Shirky) with practice (Atwood) on designing for online communities
Developers who lock themselves into a single toolchain miss out on the broader programming world
money_quotes: I quite literally used Clay Shirky's book, Here Comes Everybody: The Power of Organizing Without Organizations, as a blueprint for building Stack Overflow.
It's Clay Shirky's Internet, We Just Live In It.
It pains me to see developers who let themselves get locked into some particular toolchain ghetto, without at least peripheral awareness of what else is going on in the programming world around them.
Good programmers are interested in everything.

We always intended Stack Overflow to be a tribute to the greater craft of programming, a place where you could rub shoulders with fellow programmers from all sorts of different backgrounds and professional interests.
key_insight: Great developer communities are built on inclusive, cross-platform foundations where good programmers can learn from each other regardless of their specific technology choices.
